coredata.dc:title: Emergent carbonized polymer dots as versatile featured nanomaterial for latent fingerprints, colorimetric sensor, and photocatalysis applications
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Cheng Hao
Dhenadhayalan, Namasivayam
Lin, King-Chuen
coredata.dc:description: A facile and multifeatured nanomaterial ‘carbon dots-incorporated polyethyleneimine polymer dots’ (CPPDs) was developed for the utilization in forensic and environmental applications. The one-step hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize CPPDs in which nile blue-derived carbon dots (C-dots) were encapsulated by polyethyleneimine polymeric chains to form a core-shell-nanostructured CPPDs. By taking advantage of the excellent optical spectral properties of CPPDs, three distinct applications including latent fingerprints (LFPs), colorimetric sensors, and metal-free photocatalysis were implemented. CPPDs were employed as a fluorescent probe in the development of LFPs on various substrates. The developed LFPs showed distinct fluorescent patterns with high resolution, providing three levels of identification of a fingerprint including its core, pores, island, and bifurcations. Second, CPPDs were established as a colorimetric sensing probe for the detection of copper ions (Cu2+). When Cu2+ ions were added, the colorless CPPDs solution was turned to a blue color with a detection limit of 6.87 nM. The high selectivity and sensitivity were achieved due to the existence of abundant amine functional groups that led to a stronger interaction with copper ions resulting in complex formation. In addition to LFPs and sensor applications, CPPDs showed their proficiency as a photocatalyst for the degradation of toxic chromium [Cr(VI)] solution. The photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) was performed under visible light illumination in which nearly 94% degradation was achieved in 30 min with a rate constant of 0.1339 min−1. An effective degradation process was governed by photogenerated electrons via a reduction reaction. It is anticipated that this work will lead to the development of facile, low-cost, and effective C-dots/polymer dots-based nanomaterials for synergic applications.

head.ce:title: Emergent carbonized polymer dots as versatile featured nanomaterial for latent fingerprints, colorimetric sensor, and photocatalysis applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and multifeatured nanomaterial ‘carbon dots-incorporated polyethyleneimine polymer dots’ (CPPDs) was developed for the utilization in forensic and environmental applications. The one-step hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize CPPDs in which nile blue-derived carbon dots (C-dots) were encapsulated by polyethyleneimine polymeric chains to form a core-shell-nanostructured CPPDs. By taking advantage of the excellent optical spectral properties of CPPDs, three distinct applications including latent fingerprints (LFPs), colorimetric sensors, and metal-free photocatalysis were implemented. CPPDs were employed as a fluorescent probe in the development of LFPs on various substrates. The developed LFPs showed distinct fluorescent patterns with high resolution, providing three levels of identification of a fingerprint including its core, pores, island, and bifurcations. Second, CPPDs were established as a colorimetric sensing probe for the detection of copper ions (Cu2+). When Cu2+ ions were added, the colorless CPPDs solution was turned to a blue color with a detection limit of 6.87 nM. The high selectivity and sensitivity were achieved due to the existence of abundant amine functional groups that led to a stronger interaction with copper ions resulting in complex formation. In addition to LFPs and sensor applications, CPPDs showed their proficiency as a photocatalyst for the degradation of toxic chromium [Cr(VI)] solution. The photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) was performed under visible light illumination in which nearly 94% degradation was achieved in 30 min with a rate constant of 0.1339 min−1. An effective degradation process was governed by photogenerated electrons via a reduction reaction. It is anticipated that this work will lead to the development of facile, low-cost, and effective C-dots/polymer dots-based nanomaterials for synergic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have drawn significant interest in various fields of life science and environmental science in the past decades because of their unique photoemission properties [1–4]. Polymer dots (PDs) are emergent fluorescent materials that are formed by crosslinking of the non-fluorescent hyperbranched monomer of polymers. Generally, PDs exhibit strong photoluminescence due to having π-conjugated backbone structures as luminescence centers. Moreover, PDs possess strong photostability and biocompatibility, thereby extensively utilized as fluorescence probes in sensor and bio-applications [4–12]. On the other hand, the presence of reactive surface functional groups and/or donor-acceptor units of PDs facilitates conjugation with other molecules to form nanocomposites [13–15]. For instance, PDs conjugate with carbon-based fluorescent materials such as carbon dots (C-dots) resulting in the formation of nanohybrids named carbonized PDs or carbon-polymer dots (CPDs). These CPDs materials exhibit excellent photo-physicochemical properties compared to that of PDs, even much better than metal-based semiconducting nanomaterials in the aspects of toxicity and biocompatibility [16–19]. The polymerization and carbonization processes are generally involved in the formation of CPDs that might possess a high crosslinking structure in which carbon nanomaterials are entangled with polymer chains [17,18]. In addition, CPDs exhibit a hybrid structure of carbon core/polymer chains with plenty of functional groups on the surface, which make them unique fluorescent nanomaterials compared to other organic-based fluorescent materials.

Herein, a simple autoclave-based hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize carbon dots-incorporated polyethyleneimine polymer dots (CPPDs). Nile blue organic dye and polyethyleneimine (PEI) monomer were used as C-dots and polymer sources, respectively. The resultant CPPDs showed excellent optical properties including longer wavelength absorption with strong fluorescence features similar to reported carbonized PDs [17–19]. As reported previously, CPDs exhibited a core-shell nanostructure with a cross-linked carbonized carbon core surrounded by polymer chains [20]. However, the morphology of CPDs, especially those prepared by using bottom-up methods, was observed to be amorphous single-structured particles such as those without a clear boundary between core and shell [17]. Here, the as-prepared CPPDs showed unique structural features; for instance, the C-dots cluster located inside the particle as a core that is encapsulated by the polymeric chains as a shell structure. Apart from their excellent optical and structural properties, CPPDs exhibit versatility in their applications as follows: (1) CPPDs are used as a colorimetric sensor due to the presence of abundant amine functional groups, (2) latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection by utilizing its strong fluorescence property, and (3) CPPDs function as metal-free photocatalyst toward the degradation of a toxic substance. As previously mentioned, carbonized PDs were extensively used for bio-applications including biosensors, bioimaging, and drug delivery [21–25]. Despite its excellent applicability in biological fields, herein, the as-synthesized CPPDs were applied as a multifunctional probe in diverse applications by taking the advantage of both C-dots and PDs. It was found that the CPPDs exhibited excellent capability for the detection of copper ions (Cu2+) by the colorimetric sensing method. Generally, colorimetric-based detection methods are sensitive, selective, convenient, and easy to perform [26–28]. Copper is an important transition metal that plays a vital role in several metabolic processes in living things. It is one of the vital micronutrients needed for human life with a certain low concentration level. When the existence of a high concentration of copper ions drastically produces severe damage to cell functions in the human body, thereby causing various health effects including liver/kidney damage and neuronal-related diseases [29–32]. In addition, the contamination of copper ions due to industries and agricultural resources is creating a major threat to the environment.

Forensic science is an interdisciplinary field that helps us gather, analyze, and interpret physical evidence in criminal activity. LFPs discovery is one of the most important studies in forensic science because LFPs cannot be seen visible to the naked eye. Though, several developing methods, including physical adherence and chemical-aided visualization, were applied to make visible LFPs [33,34]. Generally, different kinds of nanoparticles (silver, gold, zinc oxide, etc.) and fluorescent nanomaterials have been utilized for developing LFPs [34–36]. Herein, CPPDs emerged as a new agent in LFPs application to achieve fluorescent patterns with high resolution. CPPDs have the ability to stain the lipid, which is one of the important components in the LFPs [37]. Moreover, CPPDs may enhance the sensitivity for detecting the lipid to generate greater contrast of luminescent fingerprints. In addition to the sensor and LFPs applications, CPPDs were utilized as a photocatalyst for the removal of toxic chromium in an aqueous solution. Hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] is one of the important pollutants to threaten the safety of environments due to its toxicity and carcinogenic effect [38–40]. So far, several metal/metal oxide-based semiconducting materials were extensively used for the removal of Cr(VI) [39–42]. Herein, to avoid transition/heavy metal-based nanomaterials, the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was performed using metal-free CPPDs as catalysts under visible light illumination in the aqueous system. The efficiency of degradation was evaluated, and the catalytic mechanism was proposed based on the scavenging experiment of reactive species.

Polyethylenimine (branched, molecular weight 600, 99%) and nile blue A perchlorate (95%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium dichromate (99.5%) and copper(II) chloride (99%) were purchased from Acros Organics. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10X, pH 7.4) was obtained from Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. The other chemicals used in this study such as inorganic compounds (metal chlorides) and organic compounds (solvents) were purchased from Acros Organics and Sigma Aldrich. Milli-Q deionized water was used in the experimental studies.

The high-resolution images of CPPDs were taken from field emission gun transmission electron microscopy (FETEM, JEOL JEM-2100F). The infrared spectra were obtained by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using the VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific, UK). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded with Bruker DPX 400 MHz NMR spectrometer using D2O as the solvent. The Thermo Scientific Evolution 220 UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to record the absorption spectrum of CPPDs. The excitation and fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer LS45 spectrometer. To acquire more detailed fluorescent LFPs, confocal fluorescence images were recorded using confocal microscopy (Zeiss Observer Z1, Zeiss LSM 710 system) under an excitation of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PEI PDs and CPPDs
ce:section.ce:para: PEI PDs were prepared by using the autoclave-based hydrothermal method. Briefly, PEI monomer (1 g) was dissolved in deionized water (7.6 mL) and used as a stock solution. Then, 1 mL of stock solution was diluted with deionized water (9 mL) followed by heating in an autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The resultant solution was centrifuged (11,000 rpm) to remove solid impurities. The obtained supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water for a week to get pure PEI PDs.

A similar approach was applied to prepare the CPPDs. First, the stock solution of nile blue (0.01 g) was prepared in deionized water (7.6 mL). Then, each 1 mL of nile blue and PEI monomer stock solution was mixed with 8 mL of deionized water. The solution mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Then, the obtained CPPDs solution was purified using centrifugation (11,000 rpm) followed by dialysis against deionized water for a week.

The quantum yield of PEI PDs and CPPDs was determined by using coumarin 480 in water as the reference with the equation shown below [43]:

where Φ r is the quantum yield of coumarin 480 (0.66); I is the integrated fluorescence intensity; A is the absorbance at 396 nm; η is the refractive index of solvent. The subscripts x and r are the sample and reference, respectively.

Several substrates including tape, cover glass, aluminum foil, copper sheet, and coin were used for the LFPs application. Initially, our group member washed hands and then rubbed a fingertip across oily parts of the body including the forehead and nose. After that, the oiled fingertip was mildly pressed on the surface of substrates. The CPPDs solution was taken in the beaker and then each fingerprint-coated substrate was immersed in the solution for 5 min. Then, the substrate was carefully taken out, followed by a rinse with distilled water, and dried at room temperature. Finally, CPPDs-treated substrates were illuminated under UV light (365 nm) and the images of fluorescent fingerprint patterns were captured by using a camera. In addition, the developed fluorescent fingerprint patterns were also monitored by using a confocal fluorescence microscope.

The sensing experiments were carried out at a pH of 7.4 conditions. A certain concentration of CPPDs stock solution was prepared in PBS buffer solution (1x, pH 7.4) and the absorbance of the solution was fixed to be ∼0.1 at 280 nm. The stock solution of various metal ions (metal chlorides, 10 mM) was individually prepared in PBS buffer solution. Then, each metal ion solution (0.5 mM) was added to a set of CPPDs stock solutions to determine the selectivity of metal ions. To evaluate the detection limit of Cu2+ ions, different concentrations of copper solution (CuCl2) were individually added to a set of CPPDs solutions (3 mL) and made up to 5 mL by PBS solution. The resultant solutions were mixed well followed by monitoring the absorption spectrum of each solution using a UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer.

The stock solution of potassium dichromate [Cr(VI), 0.05 mM] was prepared in deionized water. The pH of stock and sample solutions was maintained at ∼3. To prepare the sample solution, a certain concentration of CPPDs (0.075 mg/mL) was added into Cr(VI) solution (10 mL), and the resulting solution was stirred for 30 min under dark conditions. Then, the xenon light was used as a visible light source for continuous irradiation through the solution under stirring conditions. To eliminate UV radiation, the UV cut-off filter was placed between the light source and sample holder to obtain visible light (>400 nm, 1.0 W). The absorption spectra of sample solution were regularly monitored during the light irradiation. In the similar manner, the Cr(VI) degradation experiments were performed by varying concentrations of CPPDs (0.15, 0.25, and 1.0 mg/mL). The efficiency and rate constant of photocatalytic degradation reaction were estimated by using the following equations (1) and (2), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PEI PDs and CPPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The facile autoclave-based hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize PEI PDs and CPPDs. The physical and chemical properties of the prepared PDs were characterized by several techniques including FETEM, FTIR, and XPS. To determine the morphology and size of PDs, FETEM images were acquired as shown in Fig. 1 . PEI PDs were formed as spherical particles with an average size of ∼7 nm (Fig. 1a–c). On the other hand, CPPDs were formed as larger size particles than PEI PDs (Fig. 1d and e). The average size of CPPDs was measured to be ∼35 nm. It is noticeably seen in the FETEM image (Fig. 1e) that the CPPDs exhibit a core-shell-like nanostructure such as several C-dots agglomerated as a cluster and encapsulated by PEI polymeric chains. Initially, the C-dots were formed as small-sized particles followed by cluster formation. The clustering of C-dots might be induced by the electrostatic interaction between PEI PDs and C-dots. Thus, the agglomeration of C-dots and encapsulation of polymeric chains result in the formation of big-sized CPPDs.

The surface functional groups of PEI PDs and CPPDs were investigated using FTIR analysis. The resultant FTIR spectra of both PDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectra of PEI PDs and CPPDs show a strong absorption band at ∼3350 cm−1 and a weak band at ∼1590 cm−1 corresponding to N–H stretching and bending vibrations of amine groups, respectively. The band of C–N stretching vibration (amine) was observed at ∼1110 cm−1 in both PDs, which confirms that amine functional groups are present on their surface [44,45]. In the case of CPPDs, the longer wavenumber band was found to broaden compared to that of PEI PDs and redshifted to ∼3400 cm−1, which represents the presence of N–H stretching vibrations of amine belonging to PEI and C-dots along with O–H stretching vibration of C-dots moiety. Both PDs exhibit C–H stretching and bending vibrations at ∼2900 and 1450 cm−1, respectively. Interestingly, a new band was found at ∼1650 cm−1 in both PEI and CPPDs, which is assigned to the C=O stretching vibration of amide groups which proves the involvement of hydrogen bonding interaction [46]. This C=O band did not appear in the FTIR spectrum of PEI monomer, which suggests that the oxidation of amino groups in PEI monomer results in the formation of PEI PDs [44]. It is anticipated that the oxidative reaction in the secondary amine sites of PEI monomer could occur in the presence of oxygen during the heating process, which results in the formation of amide groups in PEI PDs. The FTIR results confirm the presence of an amine functional group in PEI PDs whereas CPPDs are composed of both hydroxyl and amine functional groups. Thus, the electrostatic interaction could occur between the amine groups of PEI PDs and the hydroxyl groups of C-dots to initiate the clustering and encapsulation of C-dots by PEI PDs resulting in the core-shell nanostructure, which is in good agreement with FETEM results.

XPS measurement was investigated to further elucidate the structural properties of PDs and obtained XPS spectra were deconvoluted as shown in Fig. 3 . The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C, N, and O elements for PEI PDs are shown in Figs. 3a–c. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum displays three peaks at 284.8, 285.9, and 289 eV corresponding to C–C, C–N, and C=O/C=N, respectively (Fig. 3a) [46,47]. The characteristic amine–N peak was observed at 400.1 eV (Fig. 3b), which confirms that PEI PDs consist of the amine functional groups [47]. The O1s XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to the organic oxygen species such as peaks at 531.5 and 532.3 eV are ascribed to the C=O and N–C–O groups, respectively (Fig. 3c). The observed peaks of oxygen species reveal the involvement of oxygen in the formation of PEI PDs [44]. Chen et al. have reported that the PEI NPs were formed due to the oxidation process when the reaction proceeded at a high temperature [44]. The atomic percentage of each element was estimated with aid of the atomic sensitivity factor and integrated area of the peak. PEI PDs composed of C, N, and O elements with an atomic percentage of 61.7, 15.8, and 22.5%, respectively, confirming the contribution of oxygen. Similarly, the atomic percentage of each C, N, and O element of CPPDs was evaluated to be 74.3, 13.0, and 12.7%, respectively. Such a high percentage of C in CPPDs compared to that of PEI PDs was achieved due to the incorporation of C-dots. The deconvoluted XPS spectra for each C, N, and O element in CPPDs are shown in Figs. 3d–f. Compared to the C element in PEI PDs, the XPS spectrum of C in CPPDs exhibits a broad peak that is deconvoluted into four peaks located at 283.4, 285.1, 286.3, and 288.9 eV (Fig. 3d). The appearance of a new peak at 283.4 eV is ascribed to C=C of C-dots carbon core. Similarly, a new peak at 398.9 eV along with an amine-N peak (400 eV) was obtained in the deconvoluted N spectrum (Fig. 3e). This new peak is assigned to the pyridinic-N component belonging to C-dots, which implies the doping of N elements in the carbon core of C-dots [47]. The XPS spectrum of O1s displays the characteristic peaks of C=O and C–O components as similarly observed in PEI PDs.

According to the characterization results, PEI PDs were formed due to the oxidation reaction of PEI monomer. The FTIR and XPS measurements validate the oxidation process involved in the formation of PEI PDs. When nile blue dye was introduced in the synthesis process, the C-dots were derived from the nile blue followed by encapsulation of C-dots by PEI PDs to form core-shell-like nanostructure of CPPDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical spectral properties of PEI PDs and CPPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of PEI PDs and CPPDs were acquired to perceive the optical spectral properties (Fig. 4 ). The absorption spectrum of PEI PDs displays a distinct absorption peak with a maximum of ∼347 nm (Fig. 4a). On the contrary, the absorption spectrum of CPPDs exhibits broad absorption with shoulder peaks at ∼290, 340, and 460 nm (Fig. 4a). Moreover, strong absorbance was observed in the UV region, and the absorption tail extended to 600 nm. These spectral changes in the absorption of CPPDs might be an encapsulation of C-dots and relatively broad size distributions. In addition, electron delocalization may enhance due to the conjugation such as lone-pair electrons belonging to nitrogen and oxygen are delocalized through the structure of PDs, which leads to a longer wavelength absorption band [48].

The excitation spectra of PEI PDs and CPPDs were monitored by varying emission wavelengths to identify an optimal excitation wavelength. As shown in Figs. 4b and c, the optimal excitation wavelengths for PEI PDs and CPPDs were found to be 354 and 395 nm, respectively. The peak maximum was slightly shifted (±3 nm) while varying emission wavelengths from 430 to 490 nm for PEI PDs and from 470 to 530 nm for CPPDs. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of each PEI PDs and CPPDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths to understand the fluorescence properties. The resultant fluorescence spectra of PEI PDs showed a high-intensity peak at 462 nm at an excitation of 360 nm (Fig. 4d). When an excitation wavelength was varied from 330 to 400 nm, the peak maximum was redshifted from ∼450 to 470 nm. On the other hand, the CPPDs exhibit significantly stronger fluorescence with a peak maximum at a longer wavelength than that of PEI PDs (Fig. 4e). Similar to PEI PDs, a less pronounced peak shift was exhibited in the fluorescence spectra of CPPDs such as the peak maximum was shifted from ∼500 to 530 nm at excited from 360 to 440 nm. The high fluorescence intensity was observed at 400 nm excitation wavelength. The strong fluorescence with a longer wavelength peak maximum is mainly due to the encapsulation of C-dots in CPPDs. It is anticipated that the variation in the fluorescence peak maximum of PEI PDs and CPPDs may be due to the polydispersity of fluorescence centers [17]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of PEI PDs and CPPDs was determined to be 5.3 and 9.1%, respectively, demonstrating their good fluorescence efficacy. The combined impact of C-dots and PEI PDs yields a high quantum yield of CPPDs.

Compared to PEI PDs, the CPPDs exhibit excellent optical spectral properties. By taking the advantage of C-dots and PEI, several applications including sensors, LFPs, and photocatalysis were performed with CPPDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LFPs by an fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para:
As mentioned in the experimental section, the fingerprint was deposited on substrates, including glass, adhesive tape, aluminum foil, copper sheet, and coin, followed by incubation in CPPDs solution. Then, the CPPDs-treated substrates were illuminated under 365 nm-wavelength UV light. Interestingly, each substrate showed strong fluorescence on the developed fingerprints (Figs. 5 and 6
). For instance, Fig. 5a shows digital images of the fluorescent fingerprint on a glass substrate. The patterns of developed fluorescent LFPs such as ridges and furrows are capable of distinguishing even without further chemical and/or thermal-based post-treatments. The ridges of fingerprint exhibit a fluorescence signal, whereas there is no fluorescence signal on furrows, which may be due to the preferential adsorption of CPPDs on the ridges instead of furrows. Both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions might be involved in the association between CPPDs and fingerprint components. Because the amine functional groups of CPPDs could strongly interact with fatty components (functional groups of fatty acids and cholesterol) of a fingerprint through electrostatic interaction. In addition, the hydrophobic interaction arises due to the hydrophobic nature of the longer carbon backbone of polymers in CPPDs and the fatty acids components of a fingerprint [49,50].

To further deep insight analysis of LFPs, some specific parts were magnified in the obtained fingerprint image. In general, three levels of identification are classified to characterize LFPs. The macroscopic features of LFPs pattern include core and deltas are referred to as level 1 while LFPs are mainly characterized by level 2 details including bifurcation, ridge ending, and island. Level 3 details such as pores and edge contours are characterized for further detailed understanding to a precise identification of fingerprints [49,50]. As shown in Fig. 5a, the clear vision of core, bifurcation, and ridge ending was seen in the magnified LFPs. This finding reveals that CPPDs fulfill the prerequisites for LFPs recognition at levels 1 and 2. Furthermore, fluorescence images of CPPDs-treated glass were recorded using a confocal fluorescence microscope at the excitation of 405 nm. The resultant confocal fluorescence images of LFPs show fluorescence emitting on the ridges rather than furrows (Fig. 5b), which strengthens the identification of LFPs by digital images. Moreover, more clear identifications of the core; island and ridge ending (levels 1 and 2) along with level 3 details (pores and scars) were acquired in the confocal fluorescence images of LFPs. The three levels details of LFPs with a high resolution provide apparent evidence to verify the identification of LFPs with good precision. Accordingly, the recognition of LFPs by CPPDs validates its practical utilization for suspect identification.

Similar to a glass substrate, the detection of LFPs was performed on other substrates, including adhesive tape, aluminum foil, copper sheet, and coin. Fig. 6 shows the digital image of LFPs on various substrates that exhibit excellent fluorescence signals on the LFPs, demonstrating that CPPDs are capable of the LFPs identification on diverse kinds of substrates. The fluorescence of CPPDs in the fingerprint patterns was identified as ridge and furrow along with a clear visualization of core and ridge ending after the magnification of LFPs. Accordingly, the CPPDs could be meritoriously utilized in the development of LFPs on any substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of copper metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing capability of CPPDs toward metal ions was investigated with the presence of diverse metal ions. Initially, a certain concentration of various metal ion solutions was individually added to the CPPDs solution. Interestingly, the CPPDs solution was turned to blue color from colorless when a Cu2+ ion solution was individually added (Fig. 7 a). This color change was visibly detected by the naked eye. On the contrary, the solution color was unchanged with the presence of other metal ions (Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+) solutions, indicating an insignificant interaction between these metal ions and CPPDs. The impact of metal ions in CPPDs solution is shown in Fig. 7a, demonstrating the high selectivity of CPPDs toward the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ ions. In general, the Cu2+ ion is a well-known open-shell system, which acts as the most effective Lewis acid in the interaction with the base molecule [51]. The Lewis acids accept a very large amount of negative charge; thus, the Cu2+ ion tends to accept lone-pair electrons from the nitrogen atoms of CPPDs, resulting in the coordination bond between Cu2+ and amino groups of CPPDs. In addition, Cu2+ has a larger electron affinity that might enhance the possibility of complex formation. Nonetheless, the selectivity of metal ions may depend on several factors including the size and charge of metal ions, coordination capability of metal ions, charge donating/accepting ability, and availability of heteroatoms (N, O) in CPPDs to coordinate with a metal ion [52,53].

For the quantitative detection of Cu2+ ions, sample solutions of CPPDs were prepared with the addition of varying concentrations of Cu2+ ions. The solution color was visibly distinguishable in which the intensity of solution color gradually increased as increasing concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. 7b). The color intensity in the solution mainly depends on the concentration of CPPDs and Cu2+. Here, the concentration of CPPDs was kept to be lower (Abs = ∼0.1 at 300 nm) to monitor the absorption spectral changes of CPPDs upon the addition of Cu2+. However, at lower concentrations (<1 μM), the appearance of blue color in solution could not be visibly seen by naked eyes due to much less intensity of color. To confirm the color formation, RGB-based absorbance values termed colorimetric absorbance were estimated from a selected area of a digital image using the following relation [54]:
A
R G B
= − γ l o g
(
R G B /
R 0
G 0
B 0
)
where ARGB is colorimetric absorbance, RGB represents the red, green, and blue values of the image, whereas R0G0B0 is the background red, green, and blue values in the absence of Cu2+, and γ is the gamma correction value (fixed to be 1). The Image-J software was used to calculate the RGB color intensity. Individual RGB values were obtained by three different parts selected in each solution to get an average value, and then standardized with (R + G + B) values to avoid the total color intensity differences between them. It was found that the CPPDs solution with initial concentrations of Cu2+ exhibited a high absorbance of B value [AB = B/(R + G + B)] compared to that of the bare CPPDs solution, validating color formation even at initial concentrations. In addition, the AB value progressively increases with increasing concentrations of Cu2+. The absorbance changes of blue value (ΔA = Ac – A0) were plotted against concentrations of Cu2+, where A0 and Ac are the absorbances of B value in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The resultant plot displays linearity as shown in Fig. 7c. From the slope of the linear plot, the lower detection limit (LOD) of Cu2+ was estimated to be 324 nM using the relation of 3σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation.

For quantitative determination of the LOD, the absorption spectra of each sample solution were measured and the resultant spectra are shown in Fig. 8 . The absorption spectrum of CPPDs was not influenced by the presence of lower concentrations of Cu2+ whereas a new peak gradually appeared at ∼275 nm upon the addition of high concentrations of Cu2+ (>20 μM) (Fig. 8a). The plot of absorbance changes (ΔA = Ac – A0) as a function of Cu2+ concentrations displays linearity as shown in Fig. 8b. Here, A0 and Ac are absorbances at 275 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively. From the slope of a linear plot, the LOD of Cu2+ was evaluated to be 6.87 nM in the linear detection range of 0–500 μM. The estimated LOD is better than those previously reported LOD values as listed in Table S1 [55–66], signifying the excellent sensing capability of CPPDs in the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ ions. Moreover, the estimated LOD values from the spectrophotometric and colorimetric absorbance are much lower than the standard limits of copper concentration in drinking water. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO) approved the maximum limit of copper concentration to be ∼20 and 30 μM in drinking water, respectively [67,68]. The LOD value determined by colorimetric absorbance was considerably higher than the quantitatively obtained LOD by using spectrophotometric absorbance values. It is anticipated that the difference in the LOD values may be due to the mismatched spectral sensitivity of the RGB imaging device.

Furthermore, according to the Benesi−Hildebrand (B–H) method, the stoichiometry and association constant were estimated for the association of Cu2+ with CPPDs. The absorbance changes 1/(Ac−A0) were plotted against 1/[Cu2+]. The resultant B–H plot displays linearity (Fig. 8c) that confirms 1:1 stoichiometry between Cu2+ and CPPDs. In addition, the ratio of intercept and slope provides an association constant value of 6.09 × 103 M−1 for Cu2+ ions, which proves the stronger association between Cu2+ and CPPDs. When the addition of Cu2+ ions to CPPDs, the Cu2+ ions have a stronger interaction with amine functional groups of CPPDs to form a CPPDs‒Cu2+ complex. This resultant complex imparts a characteristic pale blue color in an aqueous solution that can be seen by the naked eye and is also responsible for a new absorption peak at ∼275 nm, which is in good agreement with other literature [69,70]. Previously, Wen et al. have reported the cuprammonium complex formation due to interaction between Cu2+ ion and amino groups of PEI [69]. The probability of complex formation increases as increasing the addition of Cu2+ ions, that is why the blue color in the solution gradually enhances along with the increasing absorbance of a peak. Furthermore, to confirm the solution color change and absorption peak due to the CPPDs‒Cu2+ complex formation, the bare Cu2+ ion solution (500 μM) was individually prepared in the absence of CPPDs. The resulting copper solution was colorless, and also, no absorption peak at 275 nm was observed in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 8d), revealing the impact of the CPPDs‒Cu2+ complex to produce a blue color in the solution.

Furthermore, FTIR and 1H NMR measurements were conducted to validate the complex formation of CPPDs‒Cu2+. Fig. 9 displays the resultant FTIR and 1H NMR spectra of CPPDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. After incorporation of Cu2+ ions (500 μM), the N–H bending vibration band (∼1590 cm−1) was found to disappear along with a decrease in the bandwidth of the N–H stretching band (∼3350 cm−1) of amino groups (Fig. 9a). This result implies the formation of CPPDs‒Cu2+ complex, which was further confirmed by the observation of the NMR titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 9b, proton peaks at ∼2.3–3.5 ppm were observed in the bare CPPDs corresponding to the PEI skeleton [–CH2–CH2–NH–] along with amino groups. The peaks at ∼2.6 and 2.8 ppm are assigned to –N–CH2–CH2–NH2 and –CH2–NH2 units, whereas the peaks at 3.3 ppm arise due to signal from proton on the adjacent carbon of the amide group [44,71]. The proton peak belonging to the –NH2– group adjacent to –CH2–CO– units was observed in the upfield region (1.74 ppm) due to high electron density [44]. Likewise, the peaks observed in downfield regions such as signals at 7.9 and 8.3 ppm correspond to the proton of the amide group (–HN–CO–) and aromatic ring protons belonging to the N-doped carbon core due to crosslinking and carbonization processes, respectively [71]. On the other hand, the proton peak signals of CPPDs at ∼2.3–3.5 ppm gradually decreased with the increasing addition of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 9b), which is in good agreement with previous literature [46]. In addition, proton peaks at ∼2.3–3.5 ppm were slightly shifted to the downfield; for instance, the peak observed at 2.59 ppm shifted to 2.61 ppm for CPPDs‒Cu2+ (500 μM). These results infer that N atoms present in CPPDs involve in the formation of coordination bonding with Cu2+ ions (Fig. 9c). The incorporation of Cu2+ ions intends strong binding with amino groups that leads to changes in the chemical environment surrounding hydrogen nuclei. Accordingly, the findings from the FTIR and NMR experiments in addition to absorption spectral studies prove the formation of CPPDs‒Cu2+ complex.

Despite much utilization of C-dots and PDs in the field of sensors due to their excellent fluorescence properties, carbonized PDs were less utilized in the catalysis reaction. In general, semiconducting metal-based nanomaterials showed excellent catalytic performance in the degradation of toxic pollutants. In addition, C-dots are also exhibiting good capability in the catalysis application, which is emergent material as metal-free catalysts due to less toxicity compared to metal-based catalysts [72,73]. Recently, C-dots are extensively utilized either as bare C-dots or as a nanocomposite with other semiconductor materials in the catalysis field [72,74]. Because C-dots could function as electron acceptors/donors that unique feature leads them as a promising catalyst. Herein, by taking advantage of CPPDs, the photocatalytic activity of CPPDs was investigated toward the removal of Cr(VI) ions in an aqueous solution. To perform the photocatalysis measurement, the sample solution consisting of potassium dichromate [Cr(VI)] and CPPDs was illuminated by visible light (>400 nm). The degradation process was monitored using a UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer; the efficiency and rate constant for the degradation reaction were determined based on the changes in the time-dependent absorption spectra of the sample solution. Here, the pH value of the sample solution was fixed to be ∼3. Because the Cr(VI) species are present in the form of Cr2O7 2− at acidic pH conditions. This species possesses a strong oxidization ability thereby Cr(VI) can be easily reduced under acidic conditions compared to that in a neutral pH solution [75,76].

Initially, a certain concentration of CPPDs solution was added into Cr(VI) solution followed by stirring under dark conditions for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, the sample solution was illuminated by continuous visible light, and the absorption spectral changes were subsequently measured at regular periods. The absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) solution displays a longer wavelength absorption band at ∼355 nm. As shown in Fig. 10 , the absorption peak was gradually diminished with increasing illumination time, demonstrating the degradation of Cr(VI). During the degradation process, the yellow-colored solution turns colorless, indicating the complete transformation of Cr(VI). The degradation reaction reaches ∼70% efficiency within 90 min upon the addition of 0.075 mg/mL of CPPDs. When an increasing concentration of CPPDs, the rate of degradation was found to increase; for instance, the reaction completes with ∼94% efficiency in 30 min for adding a high concentration (1.0 mg/mL) of CPPDs (Fig. 10c). A similar experiment was carried out in the absence of CPPDs to validate its impact on the catalytic degradation process. It was found that an insignificant change in the absorbance of the absorption peak even for a longer illumination time (2 h), indicating no degradation occurs in the absence of a catalyst (Fig. 10a). These findings demonstrate the promising catalytic performance of CPPDs in the degradation of Cr(VI).

By using a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation, the absorption spectral changes ln(Ct/C0) were plotted against light illumination time to determine the rate constant of catalytic reaction. The slope of the resultant linear plot provides a rate constant value (Fig. 10d). The rate constant for the degradation of Cr(VI) was evaluated to be 0.0003, 0.0434, 0.0774, 0.1050, and 0.1339 min−1 for 0, 0.075, 0.15, 0.25, and 1.0 mg/mL concentration of CPPDs, respectively. In addition, the obtained rate constants are higher than those estimated with other reported metal-free-based photocatalysts (Table S2), signifying excellent photocatalytic activity of CPPDs [76–85]. It is well-known that the degradation of Cr(VI) is governed by the reduction reaction. To make sure the involvement of the reduction process, a small volume of NaOH solution was added to the light-irradiated sample solution. The colorless sample solution was changed to green color upon the addition of NaOH, demonstrating the presence of Cr(III) in the form of Cr(OH)3. This result confirms that the reduction reaction involved in the catalytic degradation of Cr(VI) results in the formation of Cr(III). When illumination of light through sample solution, CPPDs absorb light that causes the excitation of electrons thereby the photogenerated electrons are involved in the degradation process via transferring electrons to Cr(VI). As a result, Cr(VI) species are effectively reduced to a great extent.

To further confirm the mechanism, a similar photocatalytic degradation experiment was conducted in the presence of reactive species scavengers. Herein, ammonium persulfate (APS), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) were used as scavengers for electron, hole, hydroxyl radical, and superoxide, respectively. The fixed concentration of CPPDs (0.15 mg/mL) and each scavenger (0.2 mM) was mixed into Cr(VI) solution. Then, each scavenger-treated sample solution was irradiated followed by monitoring the absorption spectral changes. The degradation efficiency was evaluated for catalytic reaction with each scavenger as shown in Fig. 10e. The efficiency reached 82.8% within 60 min for the sample solution in the absence of a scavenger, whereas the efficiency was estimated to be 81.6, 80.5, 81.3, and 39.4% in the presence of BQ, EDTA, IPA, and APS, respectively. When compared to reactions without scavengers, degradation efficiency was significantly reduced in the presence of APS whereas EDTA, IPA, and BQ scavengers showed insignificant impact in the catalytic reaction. These findings confirm that photogenerated electrons are solely involved in the reduction of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CPPDs in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of CPPDs was implemented toward the detection of Cu2+ and degradation of Cr(VI) in the real water sample. The pond water, collected from our university campus, was taken as a polluted wastewater source. For the detection of Cu2+ ions, the CuCl2 solution (500 μM) was added to the pond water solution containing CPPDs. The resultant solution color was turned to blue color in the presence of CPPDs as shown in Fig. 11 a, such color transformation signifies the capability of CPPDs toward the detection of Cu2+ in the wastewater. For the photoreduction of Cr(VI), a certain concentration of Cr(VI) (50 μM) and CPPDs (1.0 mg/mL) was added to the wastewater solution. The sample solution was stirred for 30 min under dark conditions followed by the illumination of visible light. The Cr(VI) polluted solution was completely degraded at the end of the reaction (30 min) in which the yellow-colored solution became a colorless solution as shown in Fig. 11b. The degradation efficiency and rate constants were estimated to be 87% and 0.1039 min−1, respectively (Fig. S1). A similar kind of degradation efficiency with rate constant was acquired as compared to the results obtained in deionized water, validating the excellent photocatalytic performance of CPPDs in real wastewater solutions.

In summary, CPPDs were developed as multifunctional nanomaterials for diverse applications including colorimetric sensors, LFPs, and metal-free photocatalysis. A facile one-step hydrothermal process was applied to synthesize CPPDs using simple precursors. The prepared CPPDs were characterized well by several techniques; CPPDs have a core-shell-like nanostructure in which C-dots cluster encapsulated by PDs. CPPDs exhibited excellent optical properties such as longer wavelength absorption with strong emission behavior due to the incorporation of C-dots with PDs. The LFPs studies revealed that CPPDs are effective to detect LFPs with a high resolution on diverse substrates due to their bright fluorescence efficacy. Moreover, more details about the fingerprint, including core, pores, and bifurcation, could be identified by the digital magnification of fluorescent-developed fingerprints. CPPDs showed a great potential for the detection of Cu2+ ions via colorimetric assays with higher sensitivity. There was a stronger interaction between copper ions and amino groups of CPPDs resulting in the complex formation. As a result, the colorless CPPDs solution was turned to be blue color along with the appearance of a new absorption peak at 275 nm. The estimated lower detection limit (6.87 nM) signifies its excellent efficiency in the detection of Cu2+. In the third application of CPPDs, the toxic Cr(VI) substance was degraded in an aqueous solution based on the photocatalytic degradation method. The effective removal of Cr(VI) was achieved in a short time (30 min) via a reduction pathway, demonstrating the good capability of CPPDs as a photocatalyst. Based on the comprehensive performance, it is anticipated that carbonized PDs could have a prosperous future as promising nanomaterials in the field of forensic identification, detection of toxic substances, and photocatalytic water remediation.
Cheng Hao Lin: Conceptualization, Investigation. Namasivayam Dhenadhayalan: Conceptualization, Writing- Review & Original draft, Supervision. King-Chuen Lin: Writing- Review & Editing, Supervision.
